serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant plate of sauteed baby kailan with roast pork, approximately 250 g, comprising roughly 150 g of kailan and 100 g of sliced roast pork.

nutrient_source.title: Stir-fried Chinese broccoli with pork — composite estimate based on USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked pork (NDB 10219) and cooked Chinese broccoli (NDB 11116), adjusted for oyster sauce and cooking oil additions
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for sauteed baby kailan with roast pork exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for cooked pork (roasted, lean and fat, NDB 10219: ~271 kcal, 27.7 g protein, 17.1 g fat per 100 g) and cooked Chinese broccoli / kailan (NDB 11116: ~22 kcal, 1.9 g protein, 0.3 g fat, 3.7 g carbohydrate per 100 g), combined in an approximate 40% pork / 60% kailan ratio with additions for oyster sauce (~5 g per 100 g dish) and cooking oil (~5 g per 100 g dish). Final per-100 g values represent a weighted composite. Sodium elevated due to oyster sauce and soy sauce seasoning.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed vegetable and pork stir-fry dishes — analogous estimate based on GI values for Chinese broccoli / kailan (GI ~32, low-GI non-starchy vegetable) and roast pork (negligible GI contribution as a protein-fat food), per University of Sydney GI Database reference values for non-starchy vegetables and mixed pork dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for sauteed baby kailan with roast pork. GI estimated at 32 based on the dominant low-GI non-starchy vegetable component (kailan / Chinese broccoli) and the negligible glycemic contribution of roast pork (protein and fat dominant). Non-starchy vegetables are consistently classified as low-GI (GI < 55) in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as (32 × 4.2 g carbohydrate × 2.5) / 100 = 3.4, rounded to 3.6 accounting for oyster sauce sugars. Overall GL is low.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from oyster sauce and soy sauce seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sauce preparation, as the estimated sodium content of approximately 480 mg per 100 g (1200 mg per standard 250 g serving) represents more than half the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated GI of 32 and only 4.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g, this dish has a low glycemic load and is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, though sodium from sauces should be monitored for associated cardiovascular risk.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from roast pork and elevated sodium from sauces
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The roast pork component contributes approximately 2.8 g saturated fat per 100 g and the dish is high in sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in moderation and prefer lean pork cuts with reduced sauce to limit saturated fat and sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sauteed Baby Kailan with Roast Pork is a popular Chinese-influenced stir-fry dish widely served across Southeast Asia, including Thailand and Singapore. The dish features tender baby kailan (also known as baby Chinese broccoli or gai lan), a dark leafy green vegetable prized for its mild bitterness and crisp texture, stir-fried at high heat with sliced roast pork (char siu-style or plain roasted pork belly or loin). The cooking process typically involves blanching or briefly stir-frying the kailan in hot oil with garlic, then finishing with oyster sauce, light soy sauce, and a drizzle of sesame oil to create a savoury, umami-rich sauce that coats the vegetables and meat. The result is a dish with a balance of tender-crisp greens, succulent pork, and a glossy, flavourful sauce. Nutritionally, the dish provides a moderate caloric density of approximately 138 kcal per 100 g, with a good protein contribution of 12.5 g from the pork, moderate fat at 8.5 g, and low carbohydrate at 4.2 g, making it a low-glycemic-index dish (estimated GI 32). The kailan component contributes meaningful amounts of vitamin C, vitamin A, calcium, and folate. Sodium is elevated due to oyster sauce and soy sauce, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is suitable for low-carbohydrate and diabetic-friendly meal planning when consumed in appropriate portions.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for sauteed baby kailan with roast pork was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or any primary regional database. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data for cooked pork and cooked Chinese broccoli in an approximate ratio, with adjustments for sauce and oil additions. GI is estimated by analogy from University of Sydney GI Database reference values for non-starchy vegetables. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is high quality and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA SR Legacy data for cooked pork (NDB 10219) and cooked Chinese broccoli (NDB 11116) in an approximate 40:60 pork-to-kailan ratio, with additions for oyster sauce and cooking oil. These values should be replaced with direct laboratory analysis or an exact HPB Singapore Foodo entry if one becomes available. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries for the component ingredients at the level of detail available for this composite estimate. 3. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate based on the low-GI non-starchy vegetable dominant component; no direct GI measurement for this dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. The cuisine tag in the input JSON is listed as 'Thai' and the serving restaurant is 'Shrimp Prawn Seafood - Boat Quay'; this dish is Chinese in culinary origin but is served across Thai and Singaporean restaurant contexts. The cuisine field is retained as 'Thai' to reflect the input source context. 5. Halal status is marked false as roast pork is not halal. 6. The input cuisine_tags included 'Seafood Dish' and 'Prawn' which appear to be restaurant-level tags for the serving establishment rather than attributes of this specific dish; these tags have not been carried forward as they do not describe this food item.
